It is a program that converts a list of triples into natural language text. The given triples will be associated with the topic: Airport. Ensure that the generated text is fluent, grammatically correct, and maintains the meaning of the original data. There can be multiple triples given that make up a complex sentence. "Antwerp International Airport serves the city of Antwerp which is in Belgium, where the capital is Brussels."

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Triple:
    subject: str
    predicate: str
    object: str

def predict(triples: list[Triple]) -> str:
    return f"The {triples[0].predicate} of {triples[0].subject} is {triples[0].object}."

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-END
```

=======
def predict(triples: list[Triple]) -> str:
    if not triples:
        return ""
    # Use the subject of the first triple as the main entity
    main_subject = triples[0].subject

    # Helper to turn a predicate/object pair into a readable clause
    def clause(pred: str, obj: str) -> str:
        pred = pred.strip()
        obj = obj.strip()
        # Simple lexicalisations for common predicates
        if pred == "cityServed":
            return f"serves the city {obj}"
        if pred == "location":
            return f"is located in {obj}"
        if pred == "country":
            return f"is in {obj}"
        if pred == "elevationAboveTheSeaLevel":
            return f"is at an elevation of {obj} metres above sea level"
        if pred == "elevationAboveTheSeaLevelInFeet":
            return f"is at an elevation of {obj} feet above sea level"
        if pred == "runwayLength":
            return f"has a runway length of {obj} metres"
        if pred == "runwayName":
            return f"has runway {obj}"
        if pred == "operatingOrganisation":
            return f"is operated by {obj}"
        if pred.endswith("Identifier"):
            return f"has identifier {obj}"
        # Fallback generic phrasing
        return f"{pred} {obj}"

    # Build clauses for all triples
    clauses = [clause(t.predicate, t.object) for t in triples]

    # Combine clauses into a single fluent sentence
    if len(clauses) == 1:
        sentence = f"{main_subject} {clauses[0]}."
    else:
        # commas between all but the last clause, with an Oxford comma
        sentence = f"{main_subject} " + ", ".join(clauses[:-1]) + f", and {clauses[-1]}."
    return sentence
>>>>>>> REPLACE
